questions.question: Which US president delivered the famous 'I Have a Dream' speech — no wait, which president was in office when Martin Luther King Jr. delivered it?
questions.explanation: Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his 'I Have a Dream' speech on August 28, 1963, during John F. Kennedy's presidency. Kennedy was assassinated three months later in November 1963. It was Kennedy's Justice Department that was negotiating with civil rights leaders at the time. Kennedy's successor, Lyndon B. Johnson, signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Voting Rights Act of 1965.
questions.memory_hook: MLK's 'I Have a Dream' speech happened in August 1963 under JFK. Kennedy was shot 3 months later. LBJ signed the actual Civil Rights Act. History is often delivered by one person and implemented by another.

questions.question: The Meiji Restoration of 1868 transformed which country from a feudal state into a modern industrial power?
questions.explanation: The Meiji Restoration (1868) ended Japan's feudal government (the Tokugawa shogunate) and restored imperial rule under Emperor Meiji. Japan rapidly modernized — adopting Western technology, military systems, education, and industry. Within 40 years, Japan had become a major world power, defeating Russia in the Russo-Japanese War (1905). The Meiji Restoration is one of history's most remarkable national transformations.
questions.memory_hook: Meiji Restoration 1868 = Japan modernizes at warp speed. Went from samurai and rice farmers to a naval power that defeated Russia in 40 years. Japan studied what made Western countries powerful and systematically adopted all of it. Speed of transformation unprecedented in modern history.

questions.question: Which German Chancellor, who served from 2005 to 2021 and became the first woman to hold the office, was often called 'the most powerful woman in the world'?
questions.explanation: Angela Merkel served as Chancellor of Germany for 16 years (2005-2021), making her the longest-serving head of government in the EU during her tenure. A trained physicist from East Germany, she navigated the 2008 financial crisis, the European debt crisis, the refugee crisis, and the COVID-19 pandemic. She is widely regarded as one of the most consequential leaders of her era.
questions.memory_hook: Angela Merkel: German Chancellor 2005-2021. Physicist. First female Chancellor of Germany.

questions.question: Jesse Owens won four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics under Adolf Hitler. What is the historical truth behind the famous story that Hitler 'snubbed' Owens?
options: Hitler personally congratulated Owens in private after the 100m final, and Owens later said he had received a better reception in Berlin than he did from President Roosevelt when he returned to the United States

Hitler openly praised Owens during the Games and invited him to the Olympic Village for a state dinner — footage of their handshake was later suppressed by both German and American governments during the Cold War

Hitler did publicly snub Owens by leaving the stadium immediately after Owens' 100m victory to avoid shaking his hand — German newspapers documented his departure and the IOC reprimanded Hitler for the breach of protocol

The snub story is true — Hitler refused to appear at any event where Owens competed after the first day, which is why German officials had to present the medals in his place for all four of Owens's gold medals
questions.answer: Hitler personally congratulated Owens in private after the 100m final, and Owens later said he had received a better reception in Berlin than he did from President Roosevelt when he returned to the United States
questions.explanation: The 'Hitler snub' is largely a myth perpetuated by sportswriters. Owens himself said Hitler waved at him and Owens waved back. After the 100m, Hitler sent a signed photograph. Owens told reporters: 'When I passed the Chancellor, he arose, waved his hand at me, and I waved back at him. I think the writers showed bad taste in criticizing the man of the hour in Germany.' Owens was not invited to the White House by FDR (who did celebrate white athletes), and he had to enter his own New York ticker-tape parade reception through a back door. He remarked that Hitler acknowledged him but his own president did not.
questions.memory_hook: Owens himself said Hitler waved and he waved back — and that Hitler had treated him better than FDR did. Roosevelt celebrated the white athletes but didn't invite Owens to the White House. Owens entered his own NYC ticker-tape parade reception through a back door. The 'snub' story was invented by American sportswriters.

questions.question: What is the name of the governing body of international soccer (football)?
questions.explanation: FIFA (Fédération Internationale de Football Association) is the international governing body for soccer, founded in Paris in 1904. It oversees 211 member associations — more members than the United Nations. FIFA organizes the World Cup, Women's World Cup, and various other competitions. Its headquarters are in Zurich, Switzerland. FIFA has been frequently criticized for corruption, particularly surrounding World Cup host selection.
questions.memory_hook: FIFA = world soccer's governing body. 211 members — more than the UN. Founded 1904 in Paris. Has been rocked by corruption scandals, particularly around World Cup hosting bids. Qatar 2022 became the most controversial hosting decision in World Cup history.

questions.question: Which American director made 'Apocalypse Now' (1979), a Vietnam War film based on Joseph Conrad's 'Heart of Darkness'?
questions.explanation: Francis Ford Coppola directed Apocalypse Now (1979), starring Marlon Brando, Martin Sheen, and Robert Duvall. The production was notoriously troubled — typhoon, Brando arriving unprepared and overweight, Martin Sheen's heart attack, and Coppola's mental breakdown during filming. The documentary Hearts of Darkness (1991) about the making of the film is almost as famous as the film itself.
questions.memory_hook: Apocalypse Now = Coppola in Vietnam, 1979. The making of it was as nightmarish as the subject. Marlon Brando showed up unprepared and 100 pounds overweight. Martin Sheen had a heart attack. A typhoon destroyed the sets. Coppola called the film 'a metaphor for Vietnam itself' — ambitious, disastrous, and somehow transcendent.

questions.question: The song 'Imagine' (1971) was written by which musician as a vision of world peace?
questions.explanation: John Lennon wrote and recorded 'Imagine' in 1971 at his Tittenhurst Park estate in England. The song asks listeners to imagine a world without countries, religion, or possessions. It is one of the best-selling singles of all time and was voted the greatest song of the 20th century in multiple polls. Lennon was shot and killed in New York City on December 8, 1980.
questions.memory_hook: 'Imagine' = John Lennon, 1971. Written in his English mansion, which he acknowledged was ironic for a song about no possessions. Shot and killed in 1980 outside his New York apartment by a fan. The most beloved peace anthem written by a man who died violently.

questions.question: The Rothko Chapel in Houston, Texas (1971) is a unique intersection of art and spirituality. What did Mark Rothko intend for the 14 large-scale dark paintings he made specifically for the space?
options: Rothko designed the paintings to cycle through colors representing different emotional states depending on the light conditions at different times of day — each painting looks dramatically different at dawn, noon, and dusk

Rothko intended the monumental dark paintings to overwhelm viewers with pure emotional presence — describing the space as a 'place of absolute silence' where his paintings could induce a transcendent, almost religious experience

Rothko based each of the 14 paintings on a specific religious text from a different world faith, creating a visual interfaith dialogue that the viewer could only decode by reading the companion guidebook he wrote

Rothko initially designed the chapel paintings to be bright and colorful like his earlier work, but darkened them progressively as his mental health deteriorated — the dark panels are technically unfinished works he never intended to display
questions.answer: Rothko intended the monumental dark paintings to overwhelm viewers with pure emotional presence — describing the space as a 'place of absolute silence' where his paintings could induce a transcendent, almost religious experience
questions.explanation: Rothko accepted the commission from John and Dominique de Menil in 1964 and spent years working on the 14 panels, which are nearly black but contain deep purples and maroons visible as eyes adjust. He designed the lighting, insisted on specific panel arrangements, and saw the chapel as the culmination of his career — wanting viewers to feel 'enveloped' rather than simply looking at paintings. He died by suicide in 1970, a year before the chapel opened. It has become an interfaith pilgrimage site hosting world leaders including Jimmy Carter and the Dalai Lama. Rothko said his paintings were 'about tragedy, ecstasy, doom, and so on.'
questions.memory_hook: Rothko spent years on the 14 near-black panels, dying by suicide in 1970, one year before the chapel opened. The panels appear black at first glance but reveal deep purples and maroons as eyes adjust. He designed the space to envelope rather than be viewed from a distance. It became an interfaith site hosting Carter, the Dalai Lama, and others.

questions.question: The Serengeti, one of Africa's most iconic national parks, is located in which country?
questions.explanation: The Serengeti National Park is located in northern Tanzania, covering about 14,750 square kilometers. It is famous for the annual Great Migration, where over 1.5 million wildebeest, along with hundreds of thousands of zebras and gazelles, migrate in a circular route across the Serengeti and into the Maasai Mara in Kenya. The park was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1981.
questions.memory_hook: Serengeti = Tanzania. The Great Migration: 1.5 million wildebeest plus zebras and gazelles moving in a huge circle through Tanzania and into Kenya every year. The Maasai Mara in Kenya is where tourists watch predators intercept the migration at river crossings. Wildlife television was essentially invented at these river crossings.

questions.question: What is the name of the boundary between Earth's crust and mantle?
questions.explanation: The Mohorovicic discontinuity (or 'Moho') is the boundary between Earth's crust and upper mantle, located approximately 35 km below the continents and 5-10 km below the ocean floor. It was discovered by Croatian seismologist Andrija Mohorovicic in 1909 by analyzing seismic wave speeds. Below the Moho, seismic waves travel faster because of the denser mantle rock.
questions.memory_hook: Moho = crust-mantle boundary. Named after Croatian geologist Mohorovicic. Discovered by studying how earthquake waves change speed at this depth. The crust you live on is just the thin surface layer — below it is the mantle where rocks are hot enough to flow over millions of years.

questions.question: The Schwarzwald (Black Forest), a mountainous wooded region famous in German folklore and fairy tales, is located in which German state?
questions.explanation: The Black Forest (Schwarzwald) is located in the state of Baden-Württemberg in southwestern Germany, west of the Rhine River. It is known for its dense pine forests (which appear black from a distance), cuckoo clock production, the Black Forest gateau (cake), and the Baden wine region. The Rhine and Neckar rivers originate in the Black Forest. Brothers Grimm fairy tales (Hansel and Gretel, Rumpelstiltskin) were set in forests like this. The spa town of Baden-Baden and the city of Freiburg are in the region.
questions.memory_hook: Black Forest (Schwarzwald): Baden-Württemberg, southwestern Germany. Dense pine that looks black from a distance. Source of the Rhine. Cuckoo clocks, Black Forest cake, and the setting for countless Grimm fairy tales. Brothers Grimm collected stories from peasants throughout these forests.

questions.question: What is electrolysis, and what two gases does it produce when applied to water?
options: The natural flow of electricity through metal conductors, producing heat
Using electrical current to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction, splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen
The chemical process of oxidation in seawater
questions.answer: Using electrical current to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction, splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen
questions.explanation: Electrolysis uses electrical current to drive chemical reactions that would not occur spontaneously. Electrolysis of water (H₂O) splits it into hydrogen gas (H₂, at the negative electrode/cathode) and oxygen gas (O₂, at the positive electrode/anode) in a 2:1 ratio. This is used to produce 'green hydrogen' when powered by renewable electricity — potentially a key clean fuel. Electrolysis also purifies metals (copper refining), produces chlorine, and is used in electroplating.
questions.memory_hook: Electrolysis: electricity drives non-spontaneous reactions. Split water → hydrogen (cathode) + oxygen (anode). 'Green hydrogen' from electrolysis using solar power = clean fuel. Also purifies metals, plates jewelry, produces chlorine. Water is hydrogen and oxygen — you can separate them with a 9-volt battery and two pencils.
